data.title: Is The S&P 500 Mispricing Risk Or Hiding Opportunity? The Case For Holding Steady In A Split Market
data.description:
Right now, the S&P 500 is caught in the middle of two competing forces. On one hand, inflation is cooling—retail inflation just decreased 0.1% in March. That should in theory give the Fed some breathing room. But the market isn’t responding with much confidence. After hitting a high of 6147 in February, the index has slipped nearly 13% and is hovering around 5355. It’s not exactly a crash, but it’s not nothing either.

So, here’s where I land: the market’s in a holding pattern. There’s no strong conviction in either direction and I think investors are just waiting for the next real signal. Maybe it’s the Fed finally cutting rates, maybe it’s earnings surprising to the upside, or maybe it’s something we haven’t even thought of yet. Until then chasing this market feels premature and running from it seems equally short-sighted.

Is the Market Getting This Wrong?

It’s kind of is—but not in a wildly obvious way. Right now, fear is driving more of the narrative than fundamentals. Take the recent bump in gold prices—people are clearly bracing for chaos or at least volatility. But at the same time, consumer demand hasn’t cratered and companies aren’t sounding alarms about a full-on downturn. Inflation data as mentioned, is easing off. Even wholesale inflation decreased 0.04% in March. If the worst fears were actually unfolding, you’d expect the data to reflect that more clearly.

So, while I wouldn’t call the S&P cheap, I do think the sell-off has priced in a more bearish macro backdrop than we’re seeing. That gap between perception and reality could offer a small edge—but again it’s not big enough (yet) to justify pounding the table.

Reading Between the Lines

No single sector drives the S&P, but if you look at the financial sector, which anchors a big chunk of the index, you get some clues. Citigroup and other major players are due to report and the expectations are modest at best. That’s not necessarily a red flag—it’s more a sign that the Street is playing defense. Charles Schwab, for instance, is nudging investors toward “low-volatility high-quality” names, which is investor code for big boring companies with stable earnings. That suggests there’s still money to be made—but you’ve got to be selective and probably a bit patient.

Valuation Check

With the S&P trading around 19 times forward earnings, it’s not cheap, but it’s not crazy either. We’ve hovered around this level before and stuck there longer than people thought we would. Honestly, valuations like this used to raise eyebrows, but the post-COVID market has kind of rewritten the rules. If earnings don’t fall off a cliff—and so far, they’ve held up—the price isn’t outrageous. You’re not walking into a bargain, but you’re not buying at some euphoric peak either. It’s more or less priced where you’d expect in a weird, cautious market like this.

Sentiment Feels Fragile

The vibe in the news lately has tilted cautious. There’s talk of job losses—NY Fed surveys suggest people are genuinely worried about unemployment—and there’s an undercurrent of political risk with tariffs and central bank indecision adding to the noise. Meanwhile, headlines about alternative assets are picking up. First spot Solana ETF? That’s the kind of thing you usually see when traditional markets start to feel stale.

All of this is keeping a lid on investor enthusiasm. People aren’t selling everything, but they’re definitely tightening up.

The Risks You Can’t Ignore

There are plenty. Tariffs are back on the table, which could drag out inflation longer than the Fed’s willing to tolerate. If rate cuts don’t come—or if the Fed decides to keep pushing back because the data doesn’t justify it—valuation multiples could come under pressure. Plus, if the job market actually weakens (not just the perception of it) earnings estimates will have to adjust lower and that could mean more downside.

And we can’t rule out market psychology either. Once people start shifting to “protection mode” it takes a lot to get them leaning back in.

How This Could Play Out

If things go sideways and the Fed holds off on cuts while economic growth slows, the S&P could dip to around 4800. That’s not catastrophic, but it would mean more grinding losses. If the current trend holds—cooling inflation, stable-ish earnings and no major policy shock—we might end up flat-to-mildly higher by year-end. Call it 5600–5800 territory. Best case? Inflation undershoots, the Fed blinks and cuts in Q3, and we catch a melt-up pushing toward 6300. That path exists, but it’s got a lot of hurdles in the way.

That brings me to a more personal question a lot of investors are asking right now—should I just wait this out and come back when things feel safer?

I decided to run the numbers.

Trying to Time the Market? Here's What Happens When You Do

There’s always that voice in the back of your head—maybe I should sit this out and wait for a better entry point. And look, that instinct isn’t irrational. But I wanted to see how much it actually costs when people act on it. So, I ran the numbers.

What I Did

I took a $10,000 investment in the S&P 500, starting way back on January 1, 1980, and let it run through April 14, 2025. No trades, no timing, just a simple buy-and-hold scenario. Then I asked: what happens if you miss just a handful of the best days?

To find out, I looked at daily returns and removed the top-performing days—first the best 10, then the best 20, and so on, all the way to the top 60 days. The idea was simple: how badly does performance suffer if you're out of the market at exactly the wrong time?

The Punchline

If you stayed invested the whole time, your $10,000 would’ve grown to $507,125. Not bad. But skip the best 10 days? You’re down to $220,144. Miss the top 20, and you're looking at $125,877. By the time you’ve missed 60 days, your total return is barely more than $23,000—that’s less than 5% of what you’d have earned by just staying put.

Here’s the kicker: many of those huge up days came right after chaos. Think October 2008 during the financial crisis, or March 2020 when COVID hit hard. Even just a week ago, on April 9, 2025, the S&P snapped back with one of its best single-day returns in years—right after a brutal stretch of selling.

You don’t get a calendar invite before those days happen. They show up unannounced, often when things feel the worst.

So, What Do You Do With This?

This isn’t about being overly optimistic. The market can still slide further. But it’s a reminder that stepping out while you “wait for the dust to settle” often means you’re missing the very bounce that drives long-term gains.

If you're wondering whether to sell now and buy back later—it’s worth asking yourself: are you ready to miss the next April 9, 2025? Because that one day might do more for your portfolio than the next three months of careful planning.

This is why I’m not trying to get too clever with the S&P 500 here. With inflation softening and sentiment already shaky, I’m holding my ground—not because I expect fireworks tomorrow, but because I’ve seen what happens when you try to time the perfect moment and miss.

Final Word

This market isn’t falling apart, but it’s also not offering a clear path higher. It’s more like a slow grind that’s wearing on investor patience. If you’re already long, I don’t see a compelling reason to sell. And if you’re waiting for the perfect entry point—you might be overthinking it.

You can call it cautious, call it neutral—but I’ll call it what it is: Hold.

data.title: DeepSeek Blows Up Meta's AI Strategy
data.description:
Meta (NASDAQ:META) has a huge problem on its hands. What initially seemed like an ingenious plan to corner the open‑source AI market has completely backfired with the rise of Hangzhou DeepSeek Artificial Intelligence Co., Ltd (DEEPSEEK). To make matters worse, a plethora of incredibly performant Chinese models were subsequently released by tech giants like Alibaba (BABA) following DeepSeek’s success. These leading Chinese open‑source models are not only better than Meta’s, but also cheaper. Chinese firms, as it turns out, excel at creating state‑of‑the‑art models even under the hardware restrictions placed on them.

This revelation should not have been surprising in hindsight. After all, a large percentage of AI researchers at U.S. labs like OpenAI and Grok are of Chinese descent. As such, it would not be shocking that there is an order of magnitude more potential AI talent in China given the size of its engineering pool. While top Chinese tech firms have access to an unrivaled talent pool, Meta must recruit from a far smaller one. To make matters worse, the best AI engineers in the U.S. are already heavily drawn to frontier labs like OpenAI, Google DeepMind, and Anthropic.

DeepSeek and the Rise of Chinese Open‑Source AI

The meteoric rise of DeepSeek in early 2025 has completely upended Meta’s grandiose open‑source plans. The Chinese startup DeepSeek completely stunned the Western tech world by releasing the reasoning model DeepSeek-R1, which was nearly as performant as OpenAI’s best model at more than an order of magnitude lower cost and with faster inference. What’s more, DeepSeek claimed it trained its frontier model on less‑advanced chips for only about $6 million. While DeepSeek is likely not being entirely truthful about its access to advanced chips and its absurdly low training figures, it nevertheless delivered genuine breakthroughs in model efficiency.

The massive efficiency gains achieved by the relatively unknown DeepSeek proved that Chinese labs can build state‑of‑the‑art AI systems even under U.S. export controls on high‑end chips. More consequential, however, is that DeepSeek’s breakthrough set off a chain reaction among Chinese tech giants. Just weeks after DeepSeek R1’s launch, Alibaba rushed out Qwen 2.5‑max, a flagship open‑source model that matched Western frontier proprietary models and outperformed any open‑source model Meta had to offer. Likewise, nearly every other major Chinese AI player, from Baidu (BIDU) to Tencent, released powerful models to compete with or surpass the new standard set by DeepSeek.

Chinese tech firms’ ability to corner the open‑source market at frightening speed has rightfully caused Meta to panic. Not only have Chinese open‑source AI efforts one‑upped Meta’s models on performance, but they have also undercut the economics of AI research. Notably, DeepSeek’s earlier model V2 was open‑sourced at a cost of $0.14 per million tokens, roughly two orders of magnitude cheaper than some leading U.S. frontier models charging $15 per million tokens.

In other words, Meta’s strategy of open‑sourcing AI was supposed to give it an edge by harnessing community innovation. Meta would then become the infrastructure on which the open‑source community built their AI applications. Instead, it may have accelerated a race to the bottom in which Chinese firms excel at open‑source AI and drive costs down to near zero. The end result is that Meta has spent countless billions on open‑source models that few will likely use long term.

China’s AI Edge: Talent and State Backing

China’s leapfrog of Meta on the open‑source AI front is rooted in structural advantages beyond Meta’s control. First is the depth of engineering talent in China. In fact, nearly 40 percent of top AI researchers in the U.S. hail from China, reflecting China’s rigorous STEM education system that has produced an enormous pipeline of AI experts. It is often quipped that the real AI competition is U.S. Chinese versus China’s Chinese, and indeed many of the brightest minds driving AI advances at U.S. companies like OpenAI and Google DeepMind have roots in China.

Within China itself, the reservoir of talent is almost certainly far larger. As Meta vies for a relatively small pool of machine‑learning PhDs, especially as other top labs have already drained much of the AI talent in the U.S., China can draw from a far bigger base of engineers and researchers trained at its top universities. When unleashed on ambitious projects like generative AI, this talent pool can achieve world‑class results quickly and with relatively fewer resources.

Second, government support in China will likely only accelerate the country’s AI development. The Chinese state has long identified AI as a national priority and has marshaled vast resources to back it. The success of DeepSeek has further piqued the Chinese government’s interest and drawn billions more dollars in state funding toward AI initiatives and the national push for tech self‑sufficiency. Unlike in the U.S., where open‑source AI is viewed warily by authorities, Beijing actively encourages an open ecosystem to Meta’s detriment.

By promoting open source, Chinese leaders sidestep U.S. sanctions on chips and software by tapping the global developer community to improve their models. This strategy effectively turns the worldwide AI community into collaborators on Chinese‑led models. Even major U.S. tech companies like Microsoft, Perplexity, and Meta itself have built upon DeepSeek’s open‑source models to remain competitive. The U.S. export bans expected to hamper China’s AI efforts have instead motivated Chinese AI labs to focus on efficiency breakthroughs through software and algorithm optimizations. In short, Chinese firms have found ways to do more with less, in stark contrast to Meta, which must rely primarily on its own R&D spending and limited talent pool.

Meta’s Financial Strength vs. Waning AI Leadership

Ironically, Meta’s core business continues to thrive even as its AI leadership in open source is challenged. The company closed out 2024 with impressive financial results. It reported full‑year revenue of $164.5 billion, a 22 percent year‑over‑year increase. Meta’s efficiency drive and focus on profitability also paid off with $62.4 billion in net income, up 59 percent year over year. Meta translated those earnings into $52.1 billion in cash flow, giving it more than enough resources to reinvest in technology. The company’s capital expenditures totaled $39.2 billion last year, among the highest of any tech firm.

However, this large spend may rapidly turn into a negative if Chinese AI labs like DeepSeek continue to outperform Meta’s LLaMA models. The new reality of incredible performance and hyper‑efficient Chinese open‑source models raises doubts about the efficiency and payoff of Meta’s AI spending. Meta finds itself in an arms race where its competitors are not other Western giants, but rivals that can achieve greater open‑source breakthroughs with an order of magnitude less capital and inferior chip technology. The cost advantage and speed of iteration in the Chinese open‑source community threaten to wipe out the benefits Meta expected from open sourcing its own models.

Potential Path Forward

While Meta has invested heavily in AI, the company was never fully on the LLM hype train. Its chief AI scientist, Yann LeCun, is known for skepticism around large language models. Meta has spent much of its resources on alternative architectures like V‑JPEA and LCM. If LeCun is correct that LLMs will eventually hit a wall, Meta’s current predicament is far less worrisome. In that scenario, Meta could be ahead of the competition, given its extensive research into non‑generative approaches.

Still, LLMs appear far from hitting any theoretical wall. The current reinforcement‑learning trajectories of these models seem to follow new exponential growth paths. For instance, OpenAI’s o4‑mini and o3 releases have again blown past expectations. Many argue we are seeing glimpses of artificial general intelligence with these models. Even if LLMs eventually hit a plateau, their capabilities are so vast that losing out on the LLM market could still have a large negative impact on Meta, especially in the near term.

Conclusion

While Meta’s fundamental business is stronger than ever, its path to AI leadership is murky at best. The open‑source revolution Meta hoped to lead is now driven by DeepSeek and Chinese tech giants. This does not spell doom for Meta, as the company still owns some of the world’s most popular social media platforms and generates profits on a scale few can match. Moreover, its war chest and commitment to AI—especially alternative architectures—could yield long‑term results. With that said, Meta is clearly no longer leading the race in open‑source language models, both in quality and efficiency. Given this dynamic, it would be more prudent to downgrade Meta to a hold at its current $1.27 trillion valuation.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Xenon- A Later Stage Story
data.description:
Summary

Xenon (NASDAQ:XENE) is a Canadian biotech company with expertise developing medicines that target sodium and potassium ion channels. The focus of this article is on their lead asset, azetukalner (XENE 1101), which is a Kv7 potassium channel opener being developed for focal onset seizures. The ph IIb data in this indication was positive. The company should report data from two phase III studies by year end 2025. If the trials are successful, they can potentially submit an NDA to FDA. Beyond that, Xenon seeks to develop azetukalner for depression and in primary generalized tonic-clonic seizures. A “pipeline in a molecule” approach is unfolding and this is a key part of the investment thesis.

Focal Epilepsy

1.8 million US and 1.6 million EU patients experience focal onset seizures (FOS). These seizures begin on one side of the brain and are believed to be caused by abnormal electrical activity. Some patients experience an aura which precedes the seizure which can include visual and hearing disturbances. This condition can involve a loss of awareness of surroundings. Motor symptoms that follow can include muscle twitching, spasms and repetitive involuntary movements. This condition, a form of epilepsy, can negatively impact patient’s quality of life. Driving is often legally restricted until a patient is seizure free for a defined time period. Anti-seizure medicines are utilized to control the seizures although they do not cure the underlying condition.

The goal of therapy is to have patients seizure free. The standard of care treatments for focal onset seizures are mostly generic drugs with a variety of mechanisms of action. While there are many generic drugs that physicians can prescribe, Levetiracetam, Lamotrigine, and Lacosamid are commonly prescribed as first line agents. Fatigue, GI symptoms, dizziness, headache, and drowsiness are common side effects. Lamotrigine may have the best tolerability profile but dizziness, blurred vision, headaches and nausea can be troublesome for some patients.

Many patients on anti-seizure medicines need to be titrated to a higher dose to attain maximum efficacy which can take weeks to months. About half of the patients respond to monotherapy. If monotherapy does not prevent seizures, additional medicines are often added. Some trial and error may be required to find tolerable, effective medicines for each patient.

Prescription data suggest many patients are taking multiple medicines. Yet, approximately one third of seizure patients are not seizure free despite multiple medicines. Given this, there is a clear need for new medicines-- especially those with novel mechanisms.

Azetukalner

Azetukalner, is being tested in patients with focal onset seizures. The mechanism of action, a potassium (Kv7) channel opener, has been shown effective. It appears to work by modulating neuronal excitability. Ezogabine, a Kv7 opener was approved by the FDA as an adjunctive treatment for adults with focal seizures and marketed. However, it was later determined that it caused pigmentation of patient’s tissues including the retina. The medicine was withdrawn from the US market in 2017. Azetukalner is a next generation potassium (Kv7) channel opener which thus far has not shown similar tissue pigmentation issues.

Phase IIB data

The phase IIB study tested various doses of azetukalner and compared it to placebo. 285 patients completed the study. Roughly 50% of phase IIb participants were taking three or more anti seizure medicines at enrollment. The median number of failed seizure medicines at enrollment was 6 suggesting this was a refractory, hard to treat population. The primary endpoint of the study was the median percentage change in seizures frequency from baseline. Results revealed a robust dose-response and statistically significant results. For the 10mg, 20mg, 25 mg and placebo cohorts the reduction in seizures were 33.2%, 46.4%, 52.8% and 18.2% respectively. The results were statistically significant for all doses with a p<.001 for the two higher doses. Approximately one third of patients treated for more than 36 months in the OLE experienced ≥12 consecutive months without seizures suggesting sustained benefit.

The most common treatment emergent adverse events were dizziness (24.6%), somnolence (15.6%) and fatigue (10.9%). Two cases of urinary retention were reported. No new safety signals including tissue discoloration have been disclosed thus far in the open label trial which is ongoing.

One important finding was there was a significant reduction in seizures even in patients who had failed more than 6 anti-seizure medicines. Another important finding was that azetukalner showed a rapid onset of action and seizure reduction was evident at one week without the need for titration.

Phase 3

Two phase 3 double blind placebo controlled trials, X-TOLE and XTOLE2, are currently enrolling approximately 360 patients in each trial. Azetukalner will be tested as an adjunctive treatment for adult patients with focal epilepsy. Patients are permitted to take stable doses of other seizure medicines during the trial. For phase III, 15mg and 25 mg will be the doses tested. The number of seizures will be measured during a 9.5 week baseline and the primary endpoint will be the reduction in focal onset seizure frequency at week 12. Topline efficacy and safety data is expected to be released in 2H 2025.

Azetukalner and the Commercial Landscape

Azetukalner is likely to be positioned later in the treatment paradigm and used in treatment resistant patients after less expensive generics have proven ineffective. Some of the benefits are its once daily administration and quick onset of action— Meaningful efficacy was seen at early as at one week. For comparison, Lamictal and Vimpat can take several weeks to reach maximum effect.

In addition, azetukalner does not generally require titration which makes it easier to prescribe than some alternative medicines. (Titration was not part of the protocol in the trials to date.) In comparison, Xcopri, a branded medicine which is very effective and often used in refractory patients, must be cautiously and slowly titrated. There are six doses of Xcopri and titration is done at two week intervals which can make it time consuming for patients to reach an optimal therapeutic dose.

In addition, azetukalner has a unique mechanism of action that differs from other anti-seizure medicines. When multiple medicines are required, prescribers often wish to use different mechanisms.

Beyond that, depression is a common comorbidity observed in patients with focal onset epilepsy. It may be that depression results from disturbances to the brain from seizures or it may be a response to the difficulty managing the condition. Some, although not all clinical trials with ezogabine, (same mechanism as azetukalner) demonstrated improvements in depressive symptoms and anhedonia.

A differentiating factor in this crowded market may be azetukalner’s impact on mood. In the phase three trials of azetukalner, the Patient Global Impression of Change (PGI-C) is a secondary endpoint. An improvement in depression could be reflected on this measure although this is not a direct measurement of depression.

Azetukalner in Depression

Azetukalner showed a positive impact on mood when tested in patients with major depression. A small phase II study, X-NOVA, showed a dose response and an improvement in MADRS which was not statistically significant. However, on HAM-D17, another scale of depression and SHAPS, which measures ability to experience pleasure, a statistically significant improvement was noted. An impact on mood may be an important factor for prescribers. Investors should have a better understanding of azetukalner’s impact on depression when data is released from an investigator sponsored major depressive disorder (MDD) study. Data are expected 1H25.

The X-NOVA2 and 3 trials will test azetukalner 20 mg taken once daily for 6 weeks and compare it to placebo in patients with major depressive disorder (MDD). The primary endpoint will be change from baseline in the Hamilton Depression Rating Scale at week 6. Notably, in the ph 2 X-NOVA trial, there was a statistically significant impact on this depression scale in patients treated with azetukalner.

Investors should also note that depression studies have a high rate of failure relative to other indications. This is especially concerning when it occurs in phase 3 after data previously looked promising. Highly heterogeneous patient populations, complex symptoms, endpoints that may not adequately capture response and high placebo rates are often cited as causes. All that said, phase III trials often are generally powered to detect a treatment effect whereas smaller trials may not be.

Data in MDD is highly important because much of the investment thesis for Xenon centers on the “pipeline in a molecule” approach. MDD is an enormous indication with 21 million patients, many of whom have tolerability issues with existing first line medicines such as SSRIs.

Competiton

Biohaven's (BHVN) BHV-7000 is highly selective for the Kv7.2/7.3 channels. It is being tested in seizures, major depression and recently failed to meet the primary endpoint in a bipolar trial. It differs from azetukalner because it does not activate the GABA receptors. Some effective anti- seizure medicines are known to hit this target. The lack of impact on GABA may explain the absence of meaningful side effects such as dizziness and somnolence. However, the impact on efficacy remains to be established. Investors will need to wait until 2026 to see the results of the studies in focal onset seizures. If selectivity proves to be an asset, the tolerability profile of BHV-7000 may be superior to azetukalner.

Peak Sales Estimates

Azetukalner is likely to be step-edited—insurers will likely require a trial of multiple generic medicines before covering a branded medicine.

Lacosamide, reached sales of $1.5 Bn in 2020. Xcopri, a branded medicine which can be used for refractory seizure patients has been embraced by physicians since its launch in 2019. Last year, sales were approximately $300 million. Topamax, a much older seizure medicine approached $2.9 billion in sales before going generic. These figures suggest there is a sizable market for antiseizure medicines.

Pricing of azetukalner is likely to be similar to Xcopri, a recently launched branded drug (cost approximately $15,000 per year). Given this pricing, significant volume is required to reach meaningful sales.

A Jefferies analyst anticipates that if trials are successful, there may be a $1Bn opportunity in focal onset seizures and an additional $1Bn opportunity in depression. A commercial launch in 2026 in the focal onset seizure indication is possible. The ability to expand into other seizure indications and MDD is critical to support the investment thesis.

Financials

Xenon reported $754.4 million in cash on hand and burned approximately $240 m last year. The company appears adequately funded into 2027 at which time they may have revenue from commercial operations.

Risks

  1. If BHV-700 has a superior side effect profile and a similar efficacy profile, azetukalner may not be a preferred medicine by prescribers.
  2. XENE is a Canadian company and could potentially be subjected to US tariffs as they sell into the US market.
  3. Despite ph IIb success, the phase III trials could fail to meet the primary endpoint. Depression has a lower than average probability of success when compared with other therapeutic areas.
  4. Epilepsy is an indication with many generic drugs and insurers could significantly restrict use. Poor formulary placement due to cost could result in azetukalner becoming a niche medicine without significant sales.
  5. Azetukalner could fail to show an effect in depression and thus the “pipeline in a molecule” thesis may not play out.
  6. Azetukalner could show a latent safety issue such as pigmentation that could result in development or marketing being stopped.
  7. Difficulties with FDA or commercialization is a risk for all biotech companies.

Conclusions

This is a perilous time for biotech investing. There is great uncertainty in terms of how regulatory decisions will be made at the FDA. The XBI and Xenon have traded down in 2025.

Despite the broader backdrop, Xenon may be positioned fairly well. Azetukalner is a later stage asset in an indication outside the more obvious controversial areas. Moreover, it is a medicine which thus far has unequivocally positive efficacy and safety data in focal onset seizures. Given the highly statistically significant phase II results, in my view there is a high probability of ph III trials meeting their primary endpoint. Most importantly, azetukalner has the potential to address a true unmet need with a novel medicine.

A “pipeline in a molecule” approach is unfolding and is a key part of the investment thesis. Investors may wish to wait and see data in major depression to better assess the value of azetukalner.

Neurology and neuropsychiatry are areas where major pharma companies have shown interest. Pfizer acquired Biohaven for $11.6 Bn and currently markets its blockbuster migraine medicine. More recently, BMY acquired Karuna for $14 Bn and launched a novel schizophrenia medicine. J & J kicked off 2025 by acquiring IntraCellular for $14.6 Bn. Caplyta, which can be prescribed for schizophrenia and bipolar-related depression was the headliner and brought in almost $700 m in 2024. Abbvie had a more disappointing outcome with Cerevel.

Lastly, Xenon has a very interesting pre-clinical pipeline which includes a Nav1.7 sodium channel inhibitor. It is believed that NaV 1.7, 1.8, and 1.9 play a role in the regulation of pain. Dampening activity in these sodium channel may translate to pain relief clinically. Developing Nav1.7 inhibitors as an alternative to opiods has been challenging. Xenon is prepared to file an IND for a Nav1.7 sodium channel inhibitor which if proven safe and effective may have significant value.

data.title: Japanese Inflation Accelerates, Complicating BoJ's Rate Decision Amid Global Uncertainty
data.description:
By Min Joo Kang

Fresh food prices eased, yet inflationary pressures broadened in March

Consumer inflation in Japan edged down to 3.6% year on year in March (vs. 3.7% in February and market consensus) as fresh food prices stabilized modestly. Yet core inflation, excluding fresh food, accelerated to 3.2% (vs. 3.0% in February) and core-core inflation, excluding fresh food and energy, also accelerated to a 2.9% pace (vs. 2.6% in February). Both were in line with market consensus.

On a monthly basis, inflation rebounded 0.3% month on month, seasonally adjusted, in March (vs. -0.1% in February) with both goods and services rising 0.2% each. Fresh food prices declined for a second month, yet the earlier pickup in costs is now passing on to other manufactured food prices and services, such as eating out.

April Tokyo CPI inflation data will be released on Friday. Prices are set to accelerate even faster than in March. This will also complicate the BoJ’s rate decision in the near term as trade war risks increase.

BoJ watch

Downside risks to GDP are growing significantly even as inflation is accelerating. Also, Japanese yen trends are being closely watched by the US administration. We dropped our long-held May rate hike call last week, pushing it to July. We expect the BoJ to keep its policy rate unchanged at its May meeting even though inflation is still the key concern. The BoJ is likely to focus on economic uncertainty for now, just like many other central banks. The BoJ will base rate decisions on what happens with concessions made between the US and Japan and where the US tariffs policies go from here. We believe that by July, things will be clearer. Trade concessions will likely take shape by then, giving the BoJ more confidence to raise its policy rate in July. The BoJ's rate hike pace will be quite gradual. For now, we still pencil in two hikes next year - in April and October 2026.

Content Disclaimer

This publication has been prepared by ING solely for information purposes irrespective of a particular user's means, financial situation or investment objectives. The information does not constitute investment recommendation, and nor is it investment, legal or tax advice or an offer or solicitation to purchase or sell any financial instrument. Read more

Original Post

data.title: Fidelity National Services: Paying Up For Deals With Global Payments
data.description:
In the summer of 2023, I believed that Fidelity National Information Services (NYSE:FIS) had seen a very painful roundtrip transaction for Worldpay. The company sold a majority stake in Worldpay at a huge discount compared to the purchase price, which it paid in 2019.

The company is now selling the remaining shares, still at a painful price compared to the original purchase price, but a significant premium compared to the carrying book value. This is comforting, yet FIS reached a bigger deal with the buyer of its Worldpay stake to acquire another business line.

These simultaneous deals were positively received by the marketplace and will simplify the business, but it will become a messy period for some time to come, making me cautious as the market is positively surprised by the news here.

Keeping Track Of Developments

FIS announced the purchase of Worldpay in 2019 in a $43 billion deal, a deal designed to create an e-commerce payment giant, as FIS was attracted to the quicker pace of growth. It furthermore liked the ability to offer a more comprehensive set of services besides payments, including fraud detection, merchant services and loyalty programs.

FIS itself was an $8.4 billion business at the time, posting EBITDA at $3.1 billion. Worldpay generated just $3.9 billion in sales, but posted EBITDA of $1.9 billion and grew sales quite a bit quicker.

A $100 stock in 2019 initially rose a bit but fell to the $50 mark by the summer of 2023. While the business grew beyond its pro forma basis to $13.9 billion in sales in 2021, while posting EBITDA at $6.1 billion, shares have been lagging. Investors were a bit cautious despite earnings power trending above $6 per share, while net debt exceeded $18 billion.

After the company grew 2022 sales by 5% to $14.5 billion, sales were seen down in 2023, causing pressure on margins and earnings. As a result, the company incurred a $17.6 billion goodwill impairment charge related to Worldpay, as the company furthermore announced that it was looking at the strategic alternatives for that business.

A solution came quickly as the company announced an $18.5 billion deal to sell a 55% majority stake in Worldpay Merchant Solutions, with the deal price consisting out of assumed debt and the partial equity stake in Worldpay.

Overall, I pegged net debt coming down to about $10 billion, yet the deal and ownership structure remained a bit complicated. On a net basis, I believed that the impact was positive, but I lacked conviction to get involved.

A Recovery

Since my take in the summer of 2023, shares of FIS have on a net basis recovered, and have largely traded in a $70-$90 range for most of last year, now trading at $75 per share after a 10% return on April 17th.

This spike in share price came after no less than two larger deals announced on the same day. The company has reached a $6.6 billion deal to sell it a minority stake in Worldpay to Global Payments (GPN), that is ahead of taxes pegged around $900 million.

At the same time, the company announced a $13.5 billion deal to acquire the Global Payments' Issuer Solutions business, with the net purchase price coming down to $12 billion if we factor in tax benefits. On a net basis, the company will invest about $7 billion in these deals, or nearly $8 billion if we factor in selling taxes.

The purchase of the Issuer Solutions business takes place at 9 times synergized EBITDA, that is based on the net purchase price of $12 billion, for a $1.33 billion EBITDA contribution. The sale of the stake in Worldpay takes place at 10.5 times EBITDA, suggesting that pro forma EBITDA from those activities will fall by $628 million. On a net basis, pro forma EBITDA will jump by $700 million, as the company claims for pro forma gross leverage around 3.4 times.

What Are The Implications?

Earlier in February, the company reported its 2024 results. The company reported total revenues of $10.1 billion, down a lot from 2023 of course after the Worldpay business was no longer consolidated. The business reported EBITDA of $4.1 billion and adjusted earnings of $2.9 billion, equal to $5.22 per share.

Net debt stood at $10.5 billion, set to jump to $18.5 billion. With the net impact on EBITDA only having about a $700 million contribution, leverage ratios are seen around 3.5 times, temporarily getting higher, although that the business anticipates to report leverage ratios sub 3 within a year. The company guided for accretion into earnings, but failed to quantify this.

Following a 10% jump in the share price, the market value of FIS has jumped to about $3.5 billion, likely the result of the solid proceeds for the Worldpay stake. Even as this transaction marks the end of a painful roundtrip, the stake was valued at just $3.9 billion on the balance sheet, with the company now commanding a real premium. This and the purchase makes investors upbeat, as it simplifies the business, but on the downside, there are many moving parts here, while debt will increase quite a bit.

While the news likely is a positive, it will create some smoke screens for some time to come, which in combination with the move higher in the shares makes me very cautious, and in no need to chase the hares here.

data.title: Uber Stock: Why Driverless Cars And Tariffs Are Actually Tailwinds
data.description:
Introduction

There's been a lot said about Uber (NYSE:UBER) on this platform, but not everyone sees it as the kind of opportunity I believe it is, especially before the full effects of the upcoming Trump tariffs kick in. I think there are several positive catalysts that haven't gotten enough attention from the analysts and investors of Uber, and I'd love to walk you through them in this piece.

I'm rating Uber as a Strong Buy. I personally own shares of this company. I started buying a couple of months ago, before the tariffs, but right now, I see an additional opportunity for Uber besides the risks. My average cost basis is at around $66, below the current price, but I'd still be buying at today's levels without hesitation, and in this article, I will explain why I think this company deserves so much attention. I will mention the risks and disadvantages, as I take them into account as well.

A Positive Catalyst Nobody Talks About: Tariffs

One of the biggest positive catalysts that no one seems to be discussing is Trump's proposed tariffs on cars. Although previously applied 25% tariffs on imported vehicles are being questioned right now, if they stick, they could create a powerful tailwind for Uber.

When cars get more expensive and insurance gets more expensive, people will naturally shift away from owning and toward using services like Uber. Insurance costs might go up, for example because tariffs on imported car parts raise repair costs, which insurers pass on to customers. Besides the cost of the insurance, car owners should take into account parking fees (especially high in cities), regular testing, maintenance, and all kinds of bureaucracy. I haven't bought a car because I don't want to deal with all the things stated above, and I didn't even include the cost of the car itself. Using Uber is just easier, especially for those living in cities, where everything you might need is relatively close by, and you can get a cab in a matter of minutes. I know many people who have already chosen Uber instead of a car, and I think the tariffs could boost the demand for services like Uber's.

Autonomous Vehicles: Threat or Opportunity?

Many people consider Waymo and possibly Tesla in the future as a serious threat to Uber, but I see it as a possibility for Uber to gain new ground and to accelerate its growth. First of all, Uber was a first mover in ride-hailing and had some serious advantages early on. For example, very cheap consumer acquisition costs, which other companies won't have anymore. Consumers have picked their favorite companies, and right now, it's not about giving some new value to the customer. It's about taking customers from your competitors, which is usually done through a price war, which can be incredibly expensive, and there are no guarantees that Waymo or Tesla will be successful and that they will ever reach Uber's level of market establishment.

I might be very unpopular for what I am about to write, but I feel that this elephant in the room must be addressed. In my opinion, it's wrong to look at autonomous vehicles as a revolutionary innovation. At the end of the day, it's just a car.

Have you ever heard of the Technology Adoption Life Cycle? Driverless cars are now going through the first group of people - innovators. These are the ones who are willing to take on more risk and are very curious. This is far from the stage of real fast growth, which only begins when the innovation gets to the early majority.

I can't speak for everyone, but I can walk you through my own observations and what, I think, is going to happen with the adoption of this technology. I know for myself, I'm not an innovator. I'm not even at the beginning of the early majority. I'm somewhere in the middle. (And for the record, I'm Gen Z.)

My grandmother (a Boomer) and my father (Gen X) are actually earlier adopters than I am. So I don't believe age is the main factor here. It's the willingness to take risks. They would absolutely be the ones to fly to Atlanta or San Francisco just to try out Waymo cars and tell everyone about it. My grandma bought a Tesla the moment it was available in Ukraine. She was literally the first in her city to own one.

When it comes to me, I honestly don't care if the car has a driver or not, as long as it doesn't make my life better or easier. For example, drivers aren't always the most pleasant in terms of their need to discuss something, so I might prefer a driverless car in that case. But from another perspective, if I need a taxi and the driverless car will take 2 minutes longer to arrive, I'm getting in the regular car.

I hope you get the idea. And I know some people might hate this statement, but I'll say it anyway: autonomous vehicles are just cars. Lately, this technology has become so overhyped. Not that the technology is not incredible, it absolutely is. But I strongly disagree with the narrative that people will want it just because it's new, or cool, or "the future." Especially when we talk about ride-hailing. Buying a self-driving car for personal use? Sure, that's a big deal. It can free up your time, give you flexibility. But getting into a cab? I'm not so sure. At least, I personally wouldn't care that much whether there is a driver or not.

If it were much cheaper, then yes, I would choose the cheaper option. But first, we aren't sure yet how much it will cost, it's still too early to predict. Second, Uber is already bringing this technology on board, so the company didn't stay behind.

That's why I believe all the companies building these vehicles will need to partner with a platform like Uber. If I, a Gen Z who lives in a high-tech country, surrounded by engineers and developers, and being an early majority type of adopter, don't care which cab I get, then without a well-established interface like Uber, the possibility for those cars to scale isn't so big.

Uber's Competitive Advantage (and Its Flip Side)

Let's talk about some of Uber's competitive advantages and disadvantages.

First, Uber is a very capital-light business. Its capital expenditures are very low, and it's not only good for us, as for investors of this company. That's one of the biggest reasons Uber managed to scale so fast, expand to dozens of countries, and stay flexible when things changed. While other companies are building factories or dealing with physical assets, Uber is just optimizing its platform and cutting operating costs, without having to burn billions on capex. From the other point of view, this makes the business relatively easy to enter for new players, which creates tough competition.

But as I already explained, Uber has already established itself. Below, you can see a slide from the company's 2024 earnings presentation that impressed me the most when it came out. It's one of the reasons I bought the stock of this company back then. Uber continues to grow its user base, including in the U.S., and the management expects this growth to continue.

Let's not forget that Uber holds equity in some international players it's pulled out from, like DiDi (OTCPK:DIDIY) and Grab (GRAB), for example. So even in regions where it no longer operates directly, it still has exposure to growth. By the way, I just used Grab in Indonesia, and I thought the business was amazing. I even invested in it. You can read about that in my Grab article.

Besides, food delivery isn't going anywhere. Life is getting faster. People don't want to spend time grocery shopping or cooking. I genuinely believe this segment will continue to grow, and Uber is in a strong position to benefit. Not to mention, the cross-selling potential between Uber Rides and Eats that gets established through subscriptions.

Valuation

Looking at the valuation, I know it may not appear cheap on the surface. But if we look deeper, it becomes clear that Uber is not only priced quite fairly given its growth potential, but it is actually undervalued. The most impressive part is the company's PEG (price to earnings divided by growth) ratio. Uber's GAAP PEG (TTM) stands at just 0.04, while the sector median is at about 0.91, and Non-GAAP PEG (Forward) is 0.75, while the sector is at 1.54. Anything below 1 typically means that a company is growing faster than its valuation implies, and in this case, we're talking well below 1, and really close to 0, which is incredible.

Uber is now consistently delivering profitability, growing its user base, and expanding in high-margin areas like advertising and platform efficiency. The company is still growing at an enormous speed. Factor grades of Seeking Alpha support my outlook, with Uber rated A- to A+ in Growth, Profitability, Momentum, and Revisions.

The valuation grade is a C, but I think it's more a reflection of multiples like EV/Sales, which look high but don't tell the full story. PEG is the key metric here, in my opinion, and it strongly supports my bullish thesis.

To add even more confidence, Seeking Alpha's Quant Rating gives Uber a 4.77, which indicates a Strong Buy.

Risks

The main short-term risk is, of course, a consumer spending slowdown in the case of a recession. If macroeconomic conditions weaken further, higher interest rates, inflation, layoffs, etc., people will cut back on non-essential spending, and food-delivery and ride-hailing are among those. Uber isn't a luxury service per se, but it is a comfort, and that's often the first thing to go in a recession. That said, for those who want to own a car, it can be a downgrade or a more rational option, especially in big cities. So it's both a risk short term, but an opportunity long-term.

Razor-thin margins in food delivery are another factor to take into account. Uber Eats is growing, but food delivery is very hard to make profitable. Fees are capped in many cities, competition is tough (hello, DoorDash (DASH)), and users are super price-sensitive. The upside is there, but profitability in this segment still has a big question mark over it.

Competition is a constant risk. There are many competitors of Uber, for example, Lyft in the U.S., Bolt in Europe, and local companies in different countries like Gett, Grab, FreeNow, etc. Uber wins for now through scale, brand, and pricing, but keeping that advantage isn't guaranteed. A price war or aggressive subsidy campaign by a local competitor could still drag down profits and market share.

And of course, I won't ignore the dependence on external tech. Even though I don't see it as a major threat right now, it's definitely a risk to take into account. Uber doesn't own the cars. It doesn't build autonomous vehicles. If driverless tech becomes mainstream and companies like Tesla or Waymo decide to spend billions on their own ride-hailing services, Uber might find itself in a difficult situation.

Conclusion

In my opinion, Uber isn't just a ride-hailing company anymore. It's becoming a standard for how people move and order in the modern world. The risks are there, like in any business, but I genuinely believe the upside is being underestimated. The company is profitable, growing, and leaning into new segments that could significantly boost margins in the next few years. And the best part is that it doesn't need to build factories or reinvent the wheel. It already has the users, the platform, the reach, and amazing partnerships, like Waymo.

I don't see autonomous vehicles as a major threat, more like an opportunity for growth, and Uber is already positioned to benefit. Difficulties for car ownership could be a positive catalyst for ride-hailing players, and Uber is one of them. These opportunities, together with strong fundamentals and an attractive PEG ratio, make Uber stock a Strong Buy for me. I'm holding, I could be adding at the current levels, and I think Uber deserves a lot more credit than it's getting right now.

Editor's Note: This article was submitted as part of Seeking Alpha's Best Stock With A Catalyst investment competition, which runs through April 17. With cash prizes, this competition -- open to all analysts -- is one you don't want to miss. If you are interested in becoming an analyst and taking part in the competition, click here to find out more and submit your article today!

data.title: Amazon: Despite Bull Hype, Stock Price Same As 4 Years Ago
data.description:
Amazon.com, Inc. (NASDAQ:AMZN) has been and will be a market darling and sure-fire winner, if you listen to the typical analyst and investment adviser on Wall Street explain things. However, it's lofty valuation since the pandemic is still holding back the share quote.

Believe it or not, Amazon has underperformed the S&P 500 and even risk-free cash/savings investments since the middle of 2021, four years ago! And, I suspect major share gains will now be on hold in the unfolding Trump tariff war recession during 2025-26.

I will admit underlying growth in business operations has been above-average over the last four years. Yet, the price you pay for a stock is equally important in determining your final profit/loss destination as an owner. Amazon is a great example of this fact.

My newest worry about Amazon shareholders is a U.S. recession and trade war with the world will disproportionately affect business results. Why? Because Amazon is highly reliant on AWS/retail customers worldwide and low-cost retail vendors from around the world to create any type of material growth, given its sales saturation in America and massive size [passing Walmart (WMT) for net sales, including tech offerings, during Q4 2024].

If foreign consumers and cloud users boycott Amazon (because of its U.S. location and founder Jeff Bezos becoming a top Trump confidante), while prices for goods on the American website rise a noticeable amount (with tariff costs factored in), selling volumes and contract wins could actually be headed for a noticeable decline in the second half of this year.

My view is current Wall Street estimates for the business (mostly based on projections and management guidance expressed months ago) may prove dramatically overstated vs. the economic reality about to hit.

My question for level-headed investors... What if AMZN's remaining overvaluation problem translates into a flat to lower share quote several years out, given earnings and cash flow now reverse into a downtrend? Readers need to honestly and independently ask themselves what Amazon is worth using $5 EPS for 2025 in a mild recession scenario and sub-$4 levels in a deep recession outcome (my estimates).

Valuation Still Appears Unsustainable

My main concern today is the rotten probability of a recession with climbing inflation rates in 2025. In a stagflation outcome of rising interest rates (following tariff-inspired inflation higher) and flat to falling Amazon earnings, investors could be in for a rude wake-up call.

Already, the current "negative" spread between AMZN's trailing earnings yield and risk-free 1-year Treasury rates is unacceptable. At -0.95%, it's a worse setup than four years ago, to be frank. So, if earnings find trouble in 2025 and interest rates do not cooperate, the only way to rebalance this relative yield picture will be through a very significant share price drawdown.

When Seeking Alpha compares Amazon to other retailers (we can debate whether online subscriptions and AWS cloud units make the stock more of a hybrid technology company), shares still score a very rotten "D" rating for an overall valuation. The Quant Factor Valuation Grades used are highlighted below.

However you want to categorize the business model, Amazon still ranks as one of the worst Big Tech or Magnificent 7 names for actual free cash flow yield on new investment. And, operating free cash flow could plummet in the Trump tariff recession developing in April 2025.

Final Thoughts

Yes, I have written mostly bearish articles on Amazon since 2021 because the valuation of shares has made little sense to me (using 38 years of trading/investing experience). It's hard to argue my pessimism has proven way off base, with the share quote roughly the same as four years ago. My last discussion on reasons to avoid Amazon was published in August here, which warned of a recession and pullback in share buying interest.

Just like any other blue-chip equity, had you purchased Amazon on major price weakness and sold on substantial price gains, decent trading profits have been available from the largest online retailer in the world. Has the long-term share price growth story ended?

Now that a U.S. recession has started or is close to beginning in Q1 2025, investors need to think long and hard about the true upside on Amazon. The Atlanta Federal Reserve has been signaling for weeks, the January-March period will see flat to negative real GDP growth, with current estimates pictured below.

I absolutely believe Amazon's high valuation will not mix well with a recession, the tariff/boycott problems from Trump policy actions, and rising inflation/interest rates. Numerous alternative picks with 10%-15% annual sales gains forecasted do exist, having far less political risk (because of Bezos and Trump decisions), priced at lower valuations are available today. Why not invest from this group after the March to April -15% drop in the U.S. equity market?

Insiders and management seem to agree with my assessment that the valuation is too high, as over $5 billion in stock has been liquidated vs. NO insider buys reported to the SEC over the past year. If excellent growth prospects were reality, would this imbalance in insider trading be so lopsided?

How could Amazon rise in price this year? Well, I would say the only way to reverse recession risk at this stage would be for Trump to rescind all tariffs enacted since he came to office, followed by a heart-felt apology to all the citizens of the world. That may be the only way to justify paying a premium valuation for Amazon. I am not holding my breath.

I will say it is possible price could remain around $175-200 over the short-term if the Federal Reserve comes riding to the rescue and drops bank interest rates. Nevertheless, such would prove a transitory fix for the U.S. stock market and economy, as long-term interest rates (and borrowing costs) would likely spike on foreign investors selling U.S. dollar assets on the serious loss of confidence in fighting inflation.

Consequently, I will keep my Sell/Avoid rating for a 12-month outlook on Amazon. For me, a normalized valuation on declining earnings and cash flow may not appear until a stock price closer to $100 becomes reality.

Such a quote may be mathematically required to deliver reasonable long-term investment value for a business growing sales around 10% annually with flattening earnings during recession (good for a 5% to 6% earnings yield), compared to risk-free cash investment rates in the 4% to 5% range. Historically, this would be the common-sense conclusion in any stock missing the allure of a Magnificent 7 title. Assuming the quote zigzags between $150 and $200 the rest of 2025, I will not have much of an appetite for buying this mega-cap name.

Thanks for reading. Please consider this article a first step in your due diligence process. Consulting with a registered and experienced investment advisor is recommended before making any trade.

data.title: Sylogist Has Some Positive Momentum But Still Not Convincing
data.description:
Sylogist Ltd.(OTCPK:SYZLF) is a SaaS provider that offers mission-critical solutions on ERP, CRM, and accounting/payment. It serves 2k+ customers in government, non-profit, and education. In the last 5 years, the stock has not provided good returns for the shareholders, and the price has fluctuated between $6 and $10. As a Canada-based microcap, this business isn't receiving enough coverage, while the business has shown some positive momentum recently. Overall, I do like the business model and recent development. However, more time is needed to prove their market and margin capabilities for me to have long-term conviction.

The business is in a positive momentum during transition

Sylogist's 2024 Q4 earnings call has shown many positive developments. From the surface, the company's sales dipped 1% in Q4 and only grew 0.1% for the full year 2024 (65M). However, the companies' cloud transition has gone well. Saas subscription revenue grew 12.6% for the full year, and Saas NRR (net retention rate) is 108%. Bookings also grew 56% YOY. Moreover, their product perception continued to improve, with NPS growing from 51% in 2023 to 62% in 2024. Product win rates stay high at above 50%. The management is very confident that their cloud offerings will keep upward momentum and guide a Saas ARR growth rate of 20-25%.

One negative element is the added operational costs related to the transition to partner led channel as Sylogist wants to rely on third parties to sale and deliver their softwares. This requires new onboarding and support system for partners which involve more overhead costs. As a results, Adjust EBITA decreased to 16.2M and gross marking also declined 200 basis points to 60%.

Overall, I like what Sylogist is doing. It is clear that the current CEO is very capable and has ambitions and visions to grow the business. Sylogist definitely become a stronger company after him.

Sylogist's competitive advantage is still in question

While recent earnings calls have shown great progress, investors still need to have a strong long-term view to build conviction. On the positive side, I do think Sylogist has some level of competitive advantage. First, it strategically focuses on the mid-market, which includes smaller cities and municipalities. These areas are often not the areas competitors like Tyler Technologies will go to. Second, it was built based on Microsoft Dynamics 365, which may serve better with a large number of clients who also use Microsoft products. Third, their products are known to be customizable and user-friendly as they have been around since 1993.

Although the abovementioned factors did help Sylogist form a business moat, I have doubts about its durability and width. After all, Sylogist's technology isn't 5x better than their competitors, and it's not difficult to replicate. My initial thought on SaaS companies like Sylogist is that their product must be very sticky. However, it might be the opposite. As modern technologies continue to evolve, there are lots of businesses that offer data transferring and cloud switching services. I may argue that the old on-premise software is stickier since you have made big initial investments and your data is bound with hardware. Therefore, Sylogist is not really immune to churn just because they offer ERP or accounting software. If their products are too expensive or not as competent, people still will switch.

Lower margins don't help the narrative

The management has pitched their internal KPIs, such as NPS score, ARRs, bookings, and NRRs, that are all growing at a healthy pace. I do like these developments and agree their business is gaining shares in the mid-market regions (rural and suburban areas). However, the decreasing margins weakened the narrative. Isn't it true that Saas offerings are more automated and should increase gross margins? Sylogist customers often have limited or even declining budgets, and pricing and maintenance costs are often the most important factors in their buying decisions. It is possible that Sylogist intentionally lowers its pricing to gain market share (or other KPIs). If so, is their higher NPS score because of better product features or a cheaper price? I don't think the management has addressed that yet. If we look at a longer view, this is not just one quarter. Sylogist's gross margins have been dropping consistently in the last five years. It will be very concerning if this continues. To have strong conviction, I do want to see gross margins up to prove their pricing power.

Bottom line

Overall, I think Sylogist is a good microcap target for investors to keep an eye on. It has a long track record of profitability and isn't highly followed by the market. Because of the capital-light nature of Saas and its long reputation in the market, the company can deliver very decent cash flow (around 0.2 per share). Before the current CEO, Sylogist often chose to return cash to shareholders as dividends. Now, the company is focused on reinvesting cash back into business growth, which may be a pivotal event for the stock price. The current evaluation isn't high with EV/sales at three under a fast growth expectation of ARR growing 20-25% in 2025. However, I am not sure if this growth will be sustainable or if the margins will be improved. Also, serving the government is a tough business in the current environment, given all the budget cuts and political debates. There are just too many moving parts. Therefore, I am giving a neutral rating.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: AMD: This Dip Is A Gift
data.description:
Intro & Thesis

My first article on Advanced Micro Devices (NASDAQ:AMD) came out in late August 2023 with a "Sell" rating. I argued at the time that AMD was supposed to fall because of its "over-diversification" and the impact of rising interest rates. Time went, and I eventually upgraded the AMD stock first to "Hold" (December 2023), and then to "Strong Buy" (January 2025), with my latest update stating that AMD it should recover in the non-data-center business units, and it's the prospective EPS growth rates spoke of a substantial level of undervaluation. Unfortunately, since my previous article, the AMD stock slid deeper, currently trading at 58%+ off its all-time high.

Despite that underperformance amid all the recent tariff news and fears, I think AMD is a great beaten-down GARP stock to consider for the long run, as the business growth is unlikely to go anywhere, even if tariffs hit. The undervaluation looks massive given the prospective EPS expansion, and even after applying a discount to those earnings growth projections, most of the trade war and competition risks seem to be priced in. I reiterate my "Strong Buy" rating - the prolonged dip looks like a real gift for dip buyers right now.

Why Do I Think So?

My previous article couldn't cover the most recent Q4 results, and it came out before they were released. So, let's just briefly review the recent financials - maybe they were the reason for the stock's dip?

In Q4 2024, AMD's revenue of ~$7.6 billion (+24.16% YoY) beat the consensus by 1.76%, according to Seeking Alpha data, while the quarterly EPS of $1.09 was almost in line with projections (the beat was minimal at ~0.36%). What was important - now over 1/2 of the firm's sales come from the DC segment, which is contributing the majority of sales growth (+69% YoY in Q4), thanks to the "continued adoption of EPYC server CPUs and the rapid ramp-up of the Instinct AI accelerator business." In the server market, they've been gaining share throughout the year, particularly with the launch of its 5th Gen EPYC "Turin" processors.

The company closed the most recent quarter with a 35.5 per cent share of server revenue, marking a 3.7 percentage point increase year-on-year and a 1.6 percentage point rise quarter-on-quarter. Server unit share also rose by 2.0 percentage points year-on-year and 0.9 percentage points quarter-on-quarter.

Source: Fudzilla citing Mercury Research data

While the enterprise adoption keeps gaining momentum, with AMD's EPYC cloud instance usage nearly tripling on a YoY basis in Q4, the company also feels strong demand coming from cloud providers like Amazon's (AMZN) AWS, Google (GOOG), Microsoft (MSFT), and Tencent (OTCPK:TCEHY), who "launched over 100 new AMD-powered instances in Q4 alone."

The AI story for AMD truly took shape last year as they established a multi-billion-dollar Data Center AI franchise, delivering over $5 billion in revenue from its Instinct accelerators. During the earnings call, the management noted that the firm is rapidly expanding deployments of the MI300X GPUs with key partners like Meta (META) - using MI300X exclusively for its Llama 405B model on meta.ai - and Microsoft (powering GPT-4 based Copilot services). At the same time, they've already secured wins with numerous AI-focused Cloud Service Providers ((CSPs)) and enterprise partners like Dell (DELL), who "integrated MI300X into its AI factory solutions".

I still believe that the firm's growth momentum in the DC segment isn't going anywhere, no matter what Trump's and China's new policies bring. As you might have heard, both Nvidia (NVDA) and AMD now face new U.S. government requirements for licenses to export certain advanced AI chips to China (and related entities/countries). For AMD specifically, this impacts their MI308 products.

AMD disclosed in an 8-K filing that this change could result in "charges of up to approximately $800 million related to inventory and purchase commitments":

But compared to Nvidia's disclosure of $5 billion, AMD's China impact seems to be limited for its DC segment. At the same time, we know that the China Semiconductor Industry Association recently suggested that "China's import tariffs might exempt chips from fabless U.S. companies like AMD and Qualcomm (QCOM), whose chips are manufactured by TSMC (TSM) in Taiwan and thus classified as originating from Taiwan", while simultaneously potentially hitting IDMs like Intel (INTC) that manufacture in the U.S. This way, AMD seems to be more-less stably positioned to weather the coming tariff storm, in my understanding.

Anyway, I believe that export controls and tariff noise are creating just short-term headwinds, and all that has already contributed significantly to the recent sell-off in the stock, pricing in all the potential negatives on the business. The potential $800 million charge looks massive, but it seems manageable in the context of AMD's overall business and growth trajectory. This time the diversification should play in hand. The main risk now is the possibility of broader restrictions that could limit access to the large Chinese market in a more radical way, disrupting AMD's supply chains. But nothing idiosyncratic, I mean, nothing specific to AMD in here - if the restrictions come, they likely come for the peers as well.

On the other hand, AMD's key growth drivers - particularly AI and data center dominance outside of China - still remain largely intact, and that's the most important thing to keep in mind, in my opinion. The firm's recent move to utilize the TSMC plant in Arizona seems to be a smart move towards further diversification with a focus on the same DC segment, but on a different niche level.

So while the situation in China looks quite concerning, I don't believe it fundamentally calls into question my long-term bullish thesis, especially given AMD's increasing momentum in the AI and server markets globally.

AMD's pull-in of the next-generation MI350 series sampling and production ramp to mid-2025 should be a massive tailwind to the firm's top line in the next few quarters, improving AMD's competitive positioning against Nvidia's Blackwell platform and also broadening AMD's capabilities (particularly in training large models). In the longer term, the recently closed acquisition of ZT Systems should bring crucial rack-level and system design expertise in-house, thus enabling AMD to offer more complete AI solutions and accelerate time-to-market (especially so for the MI400 generation planned for calendar 2026). So in my view, the current revenue projection that we see in the consensus data may be at least fair, holding an upside in case the current fears of tariffs subside at some point in the future.

The EPS figures are projected to grow at a CAGR of slightly over 20% up to FY2029, and the stock is trading at 14.3x forward P/E (for FY2026). Looking at FY2025, the P/E of 19.16x is already at the lowest point since at least 2018, based on YCharts data:

Let's assume right away that the firm's bottom line is indeed hit hard by tariffs in the next 2 years, and the diversification doesn't help. At a 10% discount to FY2026 EPS estimate (I'm taking a forward multiple here), and only 20x in earnings, the stock's fair value is $110.88/share. That's ~25.58% above today's price. Again, this calculation assumes a very dramatic scenario and a relatively cheap exit valuation multiple. At 25x and in line with the current EPS consensus projection, the implied fair value would be $154, which is 74.42% above today's price.

Given all that, I reiterate my "Strong Buy" rating again.

Where Can I Be Wrong?

First off, I can be wrong in my assessment of tariff risks - the $800 million potential charge that AMD's management disclosed may be just the beginning if the trade war escalates from here on. While most of the geopolitical risks seem to be already reflected in AMD's depressed stock price right now, the actual outcome for the future price action is impossible to predict in the current abnormal market environment.

Also, after the Q4 came out, Goldman Sachs reiterated its "Neutral" rating on AMD because of the "near-term deceleration Data Center" segment and lower-than-expected guidance for Q1.

Indeed, AMD's management noted during the earnings call that Q1 revenue should be ~$7.1 billion (+/- $300 million), which is 30% YoY growth but a 7% QoQ decline due to seasonality. Any QoQ decline looks like a bad sign for semiconductor investors when there's a peer like NVDA. But from the same guidance, we know that for Q1, AMD's gross margin should remain stable at ~54%, while for the full year 2025, the management sounded confident in "delivering strong double-digit percentage revenue and EPS growth." So again, most risks seem to be already reflected in the stock price following the heavy YTD correction.

The Bottom Line

For me, the recent weakness of AMD stock amid all the recent tariff news seems to be a real gift. I think investors who think that the firm's rising role in enabling the "AI revolution" and rapidly expanding market shares in different niches, as well as the strength in the DC segment (despite Goldman's contrarian view here), point to a great recovery prospect in the next few years. The current stock price levels already suggest a massive undervaluation. Under quite bearish scenarios, AMD is at least 25% undervalued, while this undervaluation level might be well over 70-75% when the markets stabilize, in my view. So I don't call back my "Strong Buy" for AMD today.

Thank you for reading!

data.title: IDV: Long Term Risk Of Chasing Only Dividend Yield
data.description:
Investment Thesis

iShares International Select Dividend ETF (BATS:IDV) warrants a hold rating due to multiple suboptimal qualities compared to peer international dividend funds. While IDV offers a relatively high dividend yield, multiple peer funds have top holdings with stronger fundamentals leading to greater growth and dividend sustainability. Additionally, IDV has relatively high fees, reducing investor returns over the long term.

iShares International Select Dividend ETF - Overview and Compared Funds

IDV is a passive ETF that seeks to offer investors with high quality international companies that offer income through substantial dividend yields. The fund was created in 2007 and has 101 holdings with $4.35B in AUM. By sector, IDV is heaviest on financials (29.2% weight), followed by utilities (15.8%) and communication (11.0%). I wrote previously on the fund about a year ago and rated the ETF a hold. While the fund has outperformed the S&P 500 since then, IDV still deserves a hold for reasons discussed in this article.

For comparison purposes, other funds examined are Vanguard International High Dividend Yield ETF (VYMI), First Trust Dow Jones Global Select Dividend Index Fund (FGD), and Fidelity International High Dividend ETF (FIDI). Each of these funds track different indexes, but generally have the same objective of international diversification with substantial dividends. VYMI seeks to capture international companies that will have above-average dividend yields. I will cover more on how the fund might capture this more effectively than IDV in greater detail later. FGD is a global fund and therefore is the only fund that includes U.S. companies at 12.6% weight. Finally, FIDI seeks to include international holdings that pay and grow their dividends.

IDV ETF: Historic Performance, Expense Ratio, and Dividend Yield

IDV has a 5-year CAGR of 13.20%. While higher than FIDI’s 5-year average return of 12.39%, IDV lags both VYMI and FGD at 15.19% and 15.03%, respectively. All funds have underperformed the S&P 500 Index, which has seen a 5-year compound annual return of about 18%.

In addition to a slightly weaker total return compared to peer funds, IDV has a relatively high expense ratio. At 0.49%, IDV’s fees are higher than both VYMI and FIDI. Importantly, while IDV’s dividend yield is high, it has been declining over the past five years. I will cover more on the reasons for this later.

Expense Ratio, AUM, and Dividend Yield Comparison

IDV

VYMI

FIDI

Source: Seeking Alpha, 16 Apr 25

IDV Holdings and Key Consideration Factors

IDV has just over 100 holdings and is the most concentrated fund examined, with 27.8% weight on its top 10 holdings. By weight, IDV's top holdings include British American Tobacco (BTI), (LON:BATS), TotalEnergies (TTE), and Enel Chile (ENIC)(MI:ENEL).

Top 10 Holdings for IDV and Compared International ETFs

IDV – 101 holdings

VYMI – 1,561 holdings

FGD – 100 holdings

FIDI – 118 holdings

AU:ORG – 2.27%

Source: Multiple, compiled by author on 16 Apr 25

Looking forward, the long-term returns for IDV will be dependent on the quality of its holdings. However, quality holdings with strong fundamentals typically have low payout ratios that lead to increasing dividend yields. Conversely, holdings with poor fundamental metrics may need higher payout ratios to cover their dividend payouts, increasing the risk of a dividend cut. Unfortunately, IDV's top holdings fall into this second category of higher payout ratios with increased risk as discussed below.

Consideration #1: Holding Quality

The first key consideration is the quality of each fund’s top holdings. IDV advertises that it seeks to capture high-quality international holdings. However, it is important to delineate how “high quality” is defined. If high quality is measured by a high dividend yield, then IDV’s top holding, British American Tobacco, is a strong holding with a dividend yield over 7%. However, a company could be offering a high dividend yield but still lack a solid foundation for long-term quality. This is arguably true for British American Tobacco, which is operating in an environment of declining tobacco demand for all age groups. This decline has contributed to British American Tobacco’s -5.2% YoY revenue growth. While the company may be attractive in the short term and has seen a 48% share price increase over the past year, it has a concerning long-term outlook.

In contrast, top holdings for other funds have demonstrated higher quality. For example, VYMI’s top holding, Nestle (OTCPK:NSRGF)(SW:NESN), a multinational food and drink company, has demonstrated steady, international growth. Unlike British American Tobacco, Nestle saw positive sales growth for both developed and emerging markets last year. VYMI captures other strong holdings as well, including Novartis (NVS), a Swiss pharmaceutical company, with a 10.8% YoY revenue growth and 23.1% net income margin. Therefore, VYMI has an advantage over IDV in terms of multiple top-weighted quality holdings.

Consideration #2: Dividend Payout Ratios

Companies with solid fundamental growth will also be able to maintain sustainable dividend yields. This sustainability can be measured by a company’s dividend payout ratio, which measures the portion of profits paid as a dividend. A high payout ratio could potentially lead to dividend cuts. While British American Tobacco has a 7% dividend yield as previously discussed, the company has a relatively high payout ratio of 71.8%. As a result, the company has been unable to significantly increase its dividend over the past five years. Looking at the average payout ratio for IDV’s top five holdings, we see an average payout ratio higher than VYMI. This implies both lower dividend sustainability and lower capacity for future growth.

IDV

Payout Ratio

VYMI

Source: Compiled by Author from Multiple Sources, 16 Apr 25

On average, VYMI’s payout ratios are lower than the average payout ratio for IDV's top five holdings. While holdings like Toyota (TYO:7203) may have a lower yield, its lower payout ratio of 22.2% contributes to greater dividend growth as measured by a 3-year CAGR of 6.3%. As a result, VYMI demonstrates increased long-term dividend sustainability compared to IDV.

Consideration #3: Declining Dividend Yields

While high payout ratios do not always lead to declining dividend yields, we can plot each funds’ dividend history to see this trend. Both IDV and FGD had average payout ratios of over 50% for its top five holdings. This average payout ratio has led to the overall declining yield for IDV, with a -4.66% 5-year dividend growth CAGR.

The lower average payout ratio for VYMI, as previously discussed, has contributed to a 5-year dividend growth of 5.37% CAGR. Therefore, while a low payout ratio does not always lead to increasing dividend yields, there is a correlation of these qualities. As a result, VYMI appears much better postured to grow its dividend over the long term compared to IDV.

Current Valuation

IDV has done considerably better than peer funds and the U.S. market, over the past year with a price return of over 14%. This performance is largely driven by investors seeking global diversification and dividend yields due to recent volatility.

Even with a strong share price performance over the past year, IDV has the most attractive valuation as measured by its price-to-earnings ratio. Its performance will likely continue to be strong as long as volatility in the U.S. market persists. Additionally, an increased risk of recession will likely continue driving investors towards income-producing dividend funds. Despite these factors, IDV’s valuation alone does not drive a buy rating. The fundamental qualities of its top holdings, including high payout ratios, are reason to suspect low to negative dividend yield growth in coming years.

Valuation Metrics for IDV and Peer Competitors

IDV

VYMI

FGD

FIDI

P/E ratio

8.97

11.70

9.24

12.79

P/B ratio

0.86

1.40

0.81

1.31

Source: Compiled by Author from Each Fund’s Website, 16 Apr 25

Risks to Investors

Due to its lack of U.S. holdings, IDV primary risk factor is the opportunity cost of investing internationally compared to U.S. domestic investment. As depicted by Hartford Funds, US equities have seen a period of outperformance lasting about 14 years. Should this trend reverse, as seen in previous historic periods, international funds like IDV could see a strong boost.

On the other hand, U.S. equities could continue to see superior performance for years to come. In the short term, a key hurdle will be the 90-day pause on reciprocal tariffs which will expire in July. If the ongoing trade war can be resolved, recession and inflationary risks could ease, presenting a strong opportunity for continued U.S. equity outperformance.

Concluding Summary

While IDV has seen strong recent performance, the fund does not deserve a buy rating due to the fundamental metrics of its top companies. These metrics, including relatively high dividend payout ratios, point towards a slow decline of the fund’s dividend yield and low growth. Additionally, IDV has relatively high fees. By comparison, VYMI was the strongest international dividend fund analyzed due to its combination of growth, dividend sustainability, and low fees.
